collections.notes:
Aquired from Bioquip, unknown year. Last recorded molt was in 2021.
Lifespan is 4-7 years. Approaching end of life.

collections.commonName: Salmon Goliath Bird Eater Tarantula (King Kong)

collections.diet: Photinia, Fiddle Leaf Fig, Bramble, Oak

careTasks.label: Remove dead individuals and moldy food

collections.diet: •	Larvae eat ripe or rotting fruit and rotting hardwood.  •	Adults eat fruit; mangos are a favorite but apple, kiwi, banana, etc will work. Adults can also be fed artificial diet (Repashy Banana Cream Pie); this is a good supplement to the regular rotten wood diet of the larvae.

careTasks.label: Remove dead individuals and moldy food

collections.diet: Larvae eat ripe or rotting fruit and rotting hardwood

sops.biologyOverview: It's a cricketr and has incomplete metamorphosis. It eats HUMANS
sops.housingEnvironment: It lives in a castle in teh sky and listens to tubularbells.
sops.feedingProtocol: Feed it pork rinds on odd days and ketchup on even days.
sops.cleaningSchedule: Spray with lysol directly into its spiracles.
sops.populationCensus: Count hte population every 3 lightyears using base 7.
sops.healthSafetyNotes: Don't let it burrow under your skin.

sops.biologyOverview:
The salmon goliath bird eater is one of the largest and fastest-growing tarantulas.
Females can grow to 10 inches in diameter and can live for 15-20 years
Males are leggier ad somewhate maller bodied compared to females and live for 4-6 years
sops.housingEnvironment: Enclosure: Terrarium (16x8x10). Temperature: 70-80°F. Humidity: 60-75%. Substrate: Coco fiber.
sops.feedingProtocol: Diet: Crickets, dubia roaches, hornworm caterpillars. Frequency: Weekly
sops.cleaningSchedule: Spot clean and reomove uneaten prey. If substrate is moldy, empty and replace.
sops.healthSafetyNotes: While our tarantulas do not normally atempt to escape their enclosures, it is advisable to always have a catch cup on hand when opening tarantula  enclosures. Like most New World tarantulas, the Giant Salmon-Pink has urticating hairs that irritate the skin and eyes. Both of our SGBE will flick a lot of hairs if disturbed.

sops.speciesName: Salmon Goliath Bird Eater Tarantula (King Kong)
sops.biologyOverview:
The salmon goliath bird eater is one of the largest and fastest-growing tarantulas.
Females can grow to 10 inches in diameter and can live for 15-20 years
Males are leggier ad somewhate maller bodied compared to females and live for 4-6 years
sops.housingEnvironment: Enclosure: Terrarium (16x8x10). Temperature: 70-80°F. Humidity: 60-75%. Substrate: Coco fiber
sops.feedingProtocol: Diet: Crickets, dubia roaches, hornworm caterpillars. Frequency: Weekly
sops.cleaningSchedule: Spot clean and reomove uneaten prey. If substrate is moldy, empty and replace.
sops.healthSafetyNotes: While our tarantulas do not normally atempt to escape their enclosures, it is advisable to always have a catch cup on hand when opening tarantula  enclosures. Like most New World tarantulas, the Giant Salmon-Pink has urticating hairs that irritate the skin and eyes. Both of our SGBE will flick a lot of hairs if disturbed.

sops.biologyOverview:
The Malaysian Jungle Nymph lays the largest egg of any insect in the world.
The egg of the Jungle Nymph can take over a year to hatch! (6-8 months is more average, in good conditions)
This species (at least the females) are the second largest known insect species.
4. The thick spines on the legs of this species are used as a defense--they will kick and hiss at predators and are able to draw blood with their spines.
Before maturity, males are dark brown and beige while females are a solid beige-brown. As adults, males are smaller and brown while females are large and bright green.
Males mimic twigs while females mimic leaves.
Males are able to fly and have colorful wings; females are not able to fly but make noise with their small wings.
When molting, these insects hang from branches and use gravity to assist in their molting.
This species is nocturnal.
 Predators of the jungle nymph include lizards, birds, spiders, and wasps.
sops.housingEnvironment: Enclosure: Locked terrarium (24x24x48). Temperature: 68-85 F. Humidity: 50-70%. Substrate: No substrate unless laying eggs
sops.feedingProtocol: Diet: Photinia, Fiddle Leaf Fig, Bramble, Oak. Frequency: Weekly with spplementation
sops.cleaningSchedule: The entire cage should be cleaned every 7-10 days.
sops.populationCensus: After nymphs are transeferred to adult cage, count out 12-30 nymphs.
sops.healthSafetyNotes: Adults are very spiny, always wear leather gloves when handling.

sops.feedingProtocol: Diet: Larvae eat ripe or rotting fruit and rotting hardwood.  Adults eat fruit; mangos are a favorite but apple, kiwi, banana, etc will work. Adults can also be fed artificial diet (Repashy Banana Cream Pie); this is a good supplement to the regular rotten wood diet of the larvae.
sops.cleaningSchedule: Remove dead beetles and moldy fruit imediately. When substrate is 60% frass, replace all. Be sure to collect eggs, larvae and pupa when changing substrate.
sops.healthSafetyNotes: Adults fly randomly, be careful when opening enclosure.

sops.feedingProtocol: Diet: Larvae eat ripe or rotting fruit and rotting hardwood
sops.cleaningSchedule: Monitor for mold and pests (fungus gnats) and replace substrate when it is 60% frass. Be sure to collect larvae and pupa when changing substrate
